original_abstract:
We introduce MCTest, a freely available crowdsourced dataset designed to facilitate research in the open-domain machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling have made significant progress, they often rely on narrow-domain datasets or focus on specific sub-tasks like relation extraction. In contrast, MCTest challenges machines to answer multiple-choice reading comprehension questions based on fictional stories—a task that requires a holistic understanding of language while providing an objective and easily gradable metric for success.

The dataset consists of 500 stories and 2,000 questions, each carefully curated to be accessible to a young child. By utilizing fictional narratives, we ensure that answer retrieval is grounded specifically in the provided text rather than in external knowledge bases like Wikipedia. Furthermore, we mandate that a significant portion of the questions require information synthesis from multiple sentences, encouraging the development of models capable of causal reasoning and basic inference.

We also detail a scalable and cost-effective construction pipeline using Amazon Mechanical Turk. By implementing rigorous quality controls—including grammar pre-screening for writers and a secondary validation phase where independent workers "take" the tests—we have produced a high-quality dataset at a fraction of the cost of expert manual editing. Initial benchmarks using lexical baselines and Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) systems show that MCTest remains a formidable challenge for current algorithms, trailing significantly behind human performance. We hope this resource provides a clear benchmark for advancing the state-of-the-art in natural language understanding.
humanized_abstract:
We present MCTest, a free crowd-sourced dataset for open-domain machine comprehension tasks. While there has been tremendous progress in the semantic modeling community over the last few years and many achievements have been reported on various tasks such as visual and textual question answering, entity recognition, or relation extraction, most of these results are based on very specialized benchmarks that only cover a limited domain or a few specific tasks. Our goal is to provide a new commonly available platform for evaluating models on broader knowledge understanding. In MCTest, we train and evaluate a reading comprehension model on multiple choice questions answered based on short fictional stories. This task requires a much deeper, more interpretable understanding of language and, as a byproduct, provides a clear and easily computable metric (i.e., whether or not a correct answer is chosen) for measuring the model’s accuracy.

We have 500 training stories, 1000 validation stories and 1000 test stories which amount to 5000 validation questions and 5000 test questions and 2000 training questions for a total of 500 stories and 2000 questions. All examples are designed to be accessible for a young child. Realistic stories were chosen so that answer extraction is constrained to the text itself rather than being the result of looking up things in some knowledge base such as Wikipedia. We also require the child to do some work to answer questions by synthesizing information from at least two sentences to train models that can learn causal relationships and basic inferencing skills.

We also describe a scalable and affordable construction process for our dataset using Amazon Mechanical Turk. We implemented rigorous quality control procedures to ensure that our data is of the highest quality at the lowest possible cost. In particular, we pre-screen all grammar before passing work on to freelance writers and we have a secondary review phase where other workers “take” our tests. An initial experiment that uses lexical baselines and existing Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) systems confirms MCTest to be a difficult new challenge for state-of-the-art algorithms and that it significantly outperforms current systems. Our goal in creating MCTest is to give the AI community a concrete and unambiguously defined benchmark to help improve the state of the art in natural language understanding.

document.output:
We present MCTest, a free crowd-sourced dataset for open-domain machine comprehension tasks. While there has been tremendous progress in the semantic modeling community over the last few years and many achievements have been reported on various tasks such as visual and textual question answering, entity recognition, or relation extraction, most of these results are based on very specialized benchmarks that only cover a limited domain or a few specific tasks. We have 500 training stories, 1000 validation stories and 1000 test stories which amount to 5000 validation questions and 5000 test questions and 2000 training questions for a total of 500 stories and 2000 questions. All examples are designed to be accessible for a young child. document.input:
We introduce MCTest, a freely available crowdsourced dataset designed to facilitate research in the open-domain machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling have made significant progress, they often rely on narrow-domain datasets or focus on specific sub-tasks like relation extraction. Furthermore, we mandate that a significant portion of the questions require information synthesis from multiple sentences, encouraging the development of models capable of causal reasoning and basic inference.

By implementing rigorous quality controls—including grammar pre-screening for writers and a secondary validation phase where independent workers "take" the tests—we have produced a high-quality dataset at a fraction of the cost of expert manual editing. Initial benchmarks using lexical baselines and Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) systems show that MCTest remains a formidable challenge for current algorithms, trailing significantly behind human performance. We hope this resource provides a clear benchmark for advancing the state-of-the-art in natural language understanding.
